# Figure description: 21.jpg

**Source**: `36572_Smolianova_1984/raw_graphics/N-42-XXXIV/21.jpg`  
**Generated by**: claude-sonnet-4-6

---

## Figure Type

This is a **fakticheskogo materiala** (factual materials map) — a standard Soviet-era geological survey document type that plots the actual locations of all data collection points used in the survey. It is essentially a **survey coverage / data distribution map** showing the spatial arrangement of observation points, drill holes, geophysical measurement stations, and traverse lines across the study area at **1:200,000 scale**.

---

## Coverage and Subject

The map covers **four adjacent 1:200,000 map sheets**:
- **N-42-XXXIV**
- **N-42-XXXV**
- **M-42-IV**
- **M-42-V**

This places the coverage in **northern Kazakhstan**, consistent with the regional survey described in Report 36572. The map was compiled in **1984** (date visible upper left: *1984г*). The survey was conducted by the **Центрально-Казахстанское геолого-производственное объединение** (Central Kazakhstan Geological-Production Association) under the **Министерство геологии Казахской ССР** (Ministry of Geology of the Kazakh SSR).

The map sheet reference number visible in the upper right is **Чертеж №21** (Drawing No. 21), and there is a library/archive stamp (purple ink) in the upper right quadrant reading approximately: **Библиотека СФ РосГеолФонд Инв. № [number]**.

---

## Key Content

The map is densely populated with **hundreds of alphanumeric point labels**, traverses, and symbols distributed across the entire sheet area. The following features are discernible:

### Data Point Distribution
- **Observation/measurement stations** are shown as small dots, circles (open and filled), and labeled alphanumeric codes throughout the map. The density is highest in the central and northern portions of the map, with somewhat sparser coverage in the southwest and southeast.
- **Open circles** (larger) appear to mark **drill holes or wells (скважины)**, scattered across the map — identifiable by their larger symbol size and isolation from traverse lines.
- **Small filled dots** likely represent **geophysical measurement points** (gravity, magnetic, or seismic stations).
- **Traverses and profile lines** are visible as chains of connected points running in roughly east–west and north–south orientations, consistent with systematic geophysical profiling.

### Point Labeling System
Points are labeled with codes following a pattern such as **О3-ХХ**, **О5-ХХ**, **О1-ХХ**, **О8-ХХ**, etc., where the prefix letter-number combination likely denotes the **survey line or traverse number** and the suffix denotes the **station number** along that line. Numeric-only labels (e.g., **3507**, **3555**, **3700**, **3702**, **3879**, **3041**, **16201**, **17855**, **17000**) appear to represent **borehole or well numbers** or possibly **gravity/magnetic station identifiers**.

### Geographic and Structural Features
- Irregular **dashed lines** and **solid irregular lines** crossing the map likely represent **geological boundaries, faults, or structural contours** projected onto the base.
- The map has a **dashed boundary line** separating the upper portion (N-42 sheets) from the lower portion (M-42 sheets), reflecting the boundary between the two 1:1,000,000 parent sheets.
- A **separate inset panel** in the lower right corner covers an additional area, likely a southeastern extension of the survey (possibly sheet **M-42-V** continuation), showing additional traverse lines and point clusters.

### Survey Density Assessment
The northern half (N-42-XXXIV and N-42-XXXV) appears to have **denser coverage**, with tightly spaced traverses, suggesting more intensive geophysical profiling there. The southwestern corner has clustered point groupings that may indicate **detailed follow-up work** over a specific anomaly or prospect area.
